title: The 'Once Upon A Time ... In Hollywood' Take Cycle, Reviewed
format: Sean gets back from vacation and he and Amanda finally work through the Once Upon a Time in Hollywood discourse, then run down the fall festival lineup, a new Academy president, two industry deaths, and an interview with The Kitchen director Andrea Berloff.

segments.heading: The Once Upon a Time in Hollywood Take Cycle
segments.note: Sharon Tate, Bruce Lee, and eight days of internet discourse

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: The take cycle Sean missed while on vacation: the portrayal of Sharon Tate and the Cliff Booth/Bruce Lee fight, plus its Best Picture odds.

films.title: Django Unchained
films.note: Cited as a case where Tarantino rewriting history reads as an act of empowerment, unlike Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.

films.title: Inglourious Basterds
films.note: Shoshanna seizing control of her own fate is the other comparison point.

films.title: Reservoir Dogs
films.note: Brought up via a recent Rewatchables episode on how the dated, nasty dialogue holds up.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: Called Tarantino's masterpiece, and the Oscar-narrative reason he might finally win 25 years later.

films.title: The Farewell
films.note: The other film Sean thinks could crack Best Picture from the first eight months of the year.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Referenced for its own packed early Academy screening, which didn't guarantee a win.

films.title: The Departed
films.note: The Scorsese comp for a director winning on a later, lesser film instead of his masterpiece.

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
The Irishman
films.note: Opens the New York Film Festival; Sean missed the trailer entirely.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: NYFF's centerpiece film, Noah Baumbach's divorce drama.

films.title: Motherless Brooklyn
films.note: NYFF's closing film. Sean re-read the source novel and is confused Edward Norton made it a period piece.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: The Safdie brothers' TIFF entry.

films.title: The Souvenir
films.note: Cited by new Academy president David Rubin as one of his two favorite movies of the year.

films.title: Midsommar
films.note: Rubin's other pick.

films.title: The English Patient
films.note: Part of Rubin's resume as a casting director, credited with bringing Juliette Binoche to Hollywood.

films.title: O.J.: Made in America
films.note: Raised as a test case for Rubin's 'what is a motion picture' eligibility question.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: The other eligibility test case: does it count as Best Animated Feature?

segments.heading: In Memoriam: Rutger Hauer and D.A. Pennebaker

films.title: Blade Runner
films.note: Hauer's best-known role.

films.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer
films.note: The original film, not the show. Amanda: 'he's quite funny' in it.

films.title: Sin City
films.note: Part of Hauer's late-career revival.

films.title: Batman Begins
films.note: The other film in that revival.

films.title: Basic Instinct
films.note: Sean's favorite Hauer stretch was his early run with Paul Verhoeven, cited here.

films.title: Total Recall
films.note: Another Verhoeven film cited alongside Hauer's career.

films.title: Turkish Delight
films.note: One of two early Verhoeven films that launched Hauer's career.

films.title: Soldier of Orange
films.note: The other.

films.title: Don't Look Back
films.note: Pennebaker's Bob Dylan documentary, Sean's entry point into his work.

films.title: Original Cast Album: Company
films.note: Pennebaker's Sondheim recording-session documentary, spoofed by Documentary Now!

films.title: Monterey Pop
films.note: His concert film of career-changing sets from Jimi Hendrix, Janis Joplin, and Otis Redding.

films.title: Town Bloody Hall
films.note: A feminist-thinkers-vs.-Norman-Mailer debate film Sean calls infuriating and hilarious.

films.title: The War Room
films.note: His campaign-trail doc on Bill Clinton's team, endlessly imitated since.

films.title: Knock Down the House
films.note: Sean name-checks it from a piece he wrote on political documentaries earlier this year.

films.title: Tolkien
films.note: Amanda's verdict: one of the least interesting films she's ever seen.

films.title: Hellboy
films.note: Sean: 'seriously, what the fuck.'

films.title: Isn't It Romantic
films.note: Both agree: smart, but not funny.

films.title: The Descent
films.note: Director Neil Marshall's earlier, genuinely great horror film.

films.title: Four Weddings and a Funeral
films.note: The classic Amanda still, somehow, hasn't seen.

films.title: Where'd You Go, Bernadette
films.note: Richard Linklater's twice-delayed adaptation of Maria Semple's novel, starring Cate Blanchett.

films.title: American Factory
films.note: Sean calls it one of the best movies he's seen this year.

films.title: Luce
films.note: Naomi Watts and Tim Roth in a Sundance pickup Sean expects to be divisive.

films.title: The Nightingale
films.note: Jennifer Kent's follow-up to The Babadook, which Amanda has already ruled out watching.

films.title: The Kitchen
films.note: Her directorial debut, starring Melissa McCarthy, Tiffany Haddish, and Elisabeth Moss.

films.title: Straight Outta Compton
films.note: Her Oscar-nominated script, born from a pitch that it wasn't a movie about a band.

films.title: World Trade Center
films.note: The Oliver Stone set she credits with teaching her how movies actually get made.

films.title: Girls Trip
films.note: She caught it opening weekend to check out Tiffany Haddish before casting her in The Kitchen.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Her answer to the show's closing question: the last great thing she's seen.

TV: Amazon's Lord of the Rings series
Hulu's Four Weddings and a Funeral
Ads: The Ringer Fantasy Football podcast promo

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood

A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
